skill_tested: Defining momentum as mass times velocity
question_text: Momentum is defined as a vector quantity equal to
answer: the product of an object's mass and its velocity

choices.text: the product of an object's mass and its velocity
mass divided by velocity
the sum of mass and speed
force multiplied by time only

explanation: $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$; momentum depends on both mass and velocity direction.
common_mistake: Students define momentum using speed only or confuse it with impulse.

skill_tested: Relating impulse to change in momentum
question_text: Impulse delivered to an object equals
answer: the change in momentum of the object

choices.text: the change in momentum of the object
the object's kinetic energy at impact
mass times gravitational acceleration
the total force acting before contact

explanation: Impulse $\vec{J} = \Delta\vec{p}$; it quantifies momentum transfer during a force interaction.
common_mistake: Students equate impulse with kinetic energy or with force alone.

skill_tested: Stating momentum conservation in isolated systems
question_text: In an isolated system, the total momentum before an interaction equals
answer: the total momentum after the interaction

choices.text: the total momentum after the interaction
zero always, regardless of collision type
the total kinetic energy after the interaction
the sum of individual forces on each object

explanation: Momentum conservation holds when no net external impulse acts on the system.
common_mistake: Students assume momentum is not conserved in inelastic collisions.

skill_tested: Classifying elastic collisions by KE conservation
question_text: A collision in which kinetic energy is conserved is classified as
answer: elastic

choices.text: elastic
inelastic (momentum conserved, kinetic energy not conserved)
perfectly inelastic only
impossible in real macroscopic systems

explanation: Elastic collisions conserve both momentum and kinetic energy.
common_mistake: Students label any collision with separated objects as elastic.

skill_tested: Identifying perfectly inelastic collisions
question_text: Two carts stick together after colliding. This interaction is best described as
answer: perfectly inelastic

choices.text: perfectly inelastic
elastic (both momentum and kinetic energy conserved)
explosive separation
momentum-not-conserved

explanation: Objects that move as one after contact share a common final velocity — perfectly inelastic.
common_mistake: Students think sticking together still conserves kinetic energy.

skill_tested: Linking Newton's third law to momentum conservation
question_text: Newton's third law explains momentum conservation because internal forces between interacting objects are
answer: equal in magnitude and opposite in direction

choices.text: equal in magnitude and opposite in direction
always zero during contact
proportional to mass only
independent of contact time

explanation: Action-reaction pairs cancel as external impulses on the isolated pair.
common_mistake: Students think the heavier object exerts a larger force during contact.

skill_tested: Interpreting impulse from force-time graph area
question_text: The area under a force-time graph for a single object during a collision represents
answer: the impulse experienced by that object

choices.text: the impulse experienced by that object
the object's instantaneous velocity
the kinetic energy lost only
the acceleration at one instant

explanation: Impulse equals the integral of force over time, shown as area under an $F$-$t$ graph.
common_mistake: Students read the slope instead of the area, confusing force with impulse.

skill_tested: Justifying isolated-system approximation for carts
question_text: When analyzing a two-cart collision on a low-friction track, treating the two carts as one system is useful because
answer: external friction forces are small compared to internal collision forces

choices.text: external friction forces are small compared to internal collision forces
momentum is never conserved on tracks
each cart must always be analyzed separately
kinetic energy is always conserved on tracks

explanation: Low friction allows the pair to approximate an isolated system for momentum analysis.
common_mistake: Students assume tracks automatically guarantee energy conservation.

skill_tested: Explaining airbag design using impulse concepts
question_text: Airbags increase injury protection primarily by
answer: extending the time over which the passenger's momentum changes

choices.text: extending the time over which the passenger's momentum changes
increasing the peak force on the passenger
eliminating the need for seat belts
reducing the passenger's mass during impact

explanation: Longer $\Delta t$ for the same $\Delta p$ reduces average force via $F_{avg} = \Delta p / \Delta t$.
common_mistake: Students think airbags work by absorbing mass rather than extending collision time.

skill_tested: Applying momentum conservation to rocket thrust
question_text: Rocket propulsion in deep space demonstrates conservation of momentum because
answer: expelled exhaust gains momentum opposite to the rocket's forward motion

choices.text: expelled exhaust gains momentum opposite to the rocket's forward motion
gravity provides the forward thrust
no momentum is transferred without air to push against
only kinetic energy is conserved

explanation: Rocket + exhaust is an isolated system; total momentum remains constant.
common_mistake: Students think rockets require an atmosphere to push against.

skill_tested: Predicting equal-mass elastic head-on outcome
question_text: In a head-on elastic collision between two identical carts with equal speed and opposite direction, the carts
answer: reverse directions with the same speeds as before

choices.text: reverse directions with the same speeds as before
stop completely and remain at rest
stick together and move at half the original speed
both move in the same direction afterward

explanation: reverse directions with the same speeds as before follows from the physics principles in outcome A1.4k (Momentum and Impulse). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students predict both carts stop because forces are equal.

skill_tested: Identifying controlled variables in impulse experiment
question_text: A variable that must be controlled when comparing impulse on different foam pads is
answer: the initial speed of the object striking the pad

choices.text: the initial speed of the object striking the pad
the colour of the foam pad
the brand name printed on the pad
the room temperature only

explanation: Fair comparison requires the same incoming momentum change while varying contact material.
common_mistake: Students identify manipulated and responding variables but miss controlled variables.

skill_tested: Interpreting signed velocity in momentum calculations
question_text: When a hockey puck's velocity changes from $+8.0\ \text{m/s}$ to $-4.0\ \text{m/s}$, the sign change indicates
answer: a reversal in the direction of the momentum vector

choices.text: a reversal in the direction of the momentum vector
momentum became negative mass
the puck lost all its mass
impulse was zero because speed changed

explanation: Momentum is a vector; sign reflects direction along the chosen axis.
common_mistake: Students treat negative velocity as a separate scalar error rather than direction reversal.

skill_tested: Distinguishing impulse from kinetic energy
question_text: Comparing impulse and kinetic energy for the same collision, impulse relates to
answer: momentum change, while kinetic energy relates to work done on the system

choices.text: momentum change, while kinetic energy relates to work done on the system
both measuring exactly the same physical quantity
impulse being always larger than kinetic energy
kinetic energy determining the force-time area

explanation: Impulse and kinetic energy answer different questions about the interaction.
common_mistake: Students use kinetic energy formulas when impulse is required.

skill_tested: Classifying systems with external friction
question_text: If external friction on a sliding block is significant, the block-track system should be classified as
answer: non-isolated

choices.text: non-isolated
isolated because only two objects exist
isolated if the block is heavy
isolated if the track is level

explanation: External friction impulses prevent strict momentum conservation for the block alone.
common_mistake: Students assume two-object systems are always isolated.

skill_tested: Selecting graphical method for 2D momentum
question_text: In a two-dimensional collision analysis, a scaled vector diagram can be used instead of sine and cosine laws when
answer: the diagram is drawn carefully to scale and answers need only approximate graphical results

choices.text: the diagram is drawn carefully to scale and answers need only approximate graphical results
no vectors are involved in 2D collisions
momentum is not a vector quantity
2D collisions never conserve momentum

explanation: Alberta POS allows graphical vector addition for 2D momentum problems.
common_mistake: Students think 2D momentum problems always require component equations.

skill_tested: Relating helmet design to impulse and force reduction
question_text: Sporting helmets reduce concussion risk by
answer: increasing the collision time so the brain experiences lower average force for the same momentum change

choices.text: increasing the collision time so the brain experiences lower average force for the same momentum change
decreasing the player's mass during impact
eliminating all momentum transfer to the head
converting momentum into gravitational potential energy

explanation: Same $\Delta p$ over longer time reduces peak acceleration experienced by the brain.
common_mistake: Students think helmets prevent all momentum transfer rather than extend contact time.

skill_tested: Interpreting inelastic collision speed graph slope
question_text: The slope of a line on a graph of post-collision speed versus pre-collision speed for inelastic cart collisions is typically
answer: less than 1 and positive

choices.text: less than 1 and positive
exactly 2 for all collisions
negative for all inelastic cases
undefined because momentum is not conserved

explanation: In inelastic collisions, final speed magnitude is less than incoming speed for the striking cart.
common_mistake: Students expect slope 1 because momentum is conserved.

skill_tested: Calculating momentum change for rebounding ball
question_text: A $0.50\ \text{kg}$ ball moving at $12\ \text{m/s}$ strikes a wall and rebounds at $8.0\ \text{m/s}$ in the opposite direction. What is the magnitude of the momentum change in $\text{kg·m/s}$? Record as a whole number.
common_mistake: Students subtract speeds without accounting for direction reversal (getting 2 instead of 10).

skill_tested: Computing speed from momentum and mass
question_text: A hockey puck of mass $0.17\ \text{kg}$ has momentum $1.02\ \text{kg·m/s}$. What is its speed in $\text{m/s}$? Record to one decimal place.
explanation: Apply the relationship from outcome A1.1k in Momentum and Impulse. Substituting the given values yields 6.0, with consistent units and significant figures.
common_mistake: Students multiply mass and momentum instead of dividing.

skill_tested: Stating Coulomb's law dependence on distance
question_text: According to Coulomb's law, the magnitude of the electric force between two point charges varies
answer: inversely with the square of the distance between them

choices.text: inversely with the square of the distance between them
directly with distance
inversely with distance only
with the cube of the distance

explanation: $F = k|q_1 q_2|/r^2$; doubling separation reduces force to one quarter.
common_mistake: Students forget the inverse-square dependence and treat force as $1/r$.

skill_tested: Predicting force change when charge separation doubles
question_text: Two identical positive charges are fixed at a separation of $0.20\ \text{m}$. If the separation is increased to $0.40\ \text{m}$ while charges remain unchanged, the electric force between them becomes
answer: one quarter of the original force

choices.text: one quarter of the original force
one half of the original force
double the original force
four times the original force

explanation: Force scales as $1/r^2$; doubling $r$ gives $(1/2)^2 = 1/4$.
common_mistake: Students halve the force instead of squaring the distance ratio.

skill_tested: Explaining charging by friction via electron transfer
question_text: Rubbing a glass rod with silk leaves the rod positively charged because
answer: electrons transfer from the rod to the silk

choices.text: electrons transfer from the rod to the silk
protons move from the silk onto the rod
neutrons are removed from the rod surface
the rod gains positive charge from the air

explanation: Charging by friction involves electron transfer; the rod loses electrons and becomes positive.
common_mistake: Students think positive charge means protons move in everyday charging.

skill_tested: Identifying charging by conduction
question_text: A neutral metal sphere is touched by a negatively charged rod. After contact, the sphere becomes
answer: negatively charged by conduction

choices.text: negatively charged by conduction
positively charged by induction
polarized but still neutral overall
uncharged because metals cannot hold charge

explanation: Conduction shares excess charge; electrons spread onto the neutral conductor.
common_mistake: Students confuse conduction with induction and predict opposite charge.

skill_tested: Describing correct steps for charging by induction
question_text: In charging by induction, a charged object is brought near but does not touch a conductor that is
answer: grounded and then disconnected from ground before the charged object is removed

choices.text: grounded and then disconnected from ground before the charged object is removed
grounded throughout the entire process with no disconnection
left ungrounded with no contact ever made
touched directly by the charged object

explanation: Grounding allows charge redistribution; removing ground before removing the inducing charge traps the induced charge.
common_mistake: Students remove the inducing charge before disconnecting ground, neutralizing the conductor.

skill_tested: Defining electric field as force per unit charge
question_text: The electric field at a point in space is defined as the
answer: electric force per unit positive test charge at that point

choices.text: electric force per unit positive test charge at that point
total charge divided by distance
magnetic force on a moving electron
work done per unit current

explanation: electric force per unit positive test charge at that point follows from the physics principles in outcome B2.6k (Forces and Fields). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students define field using total force without dividing by test charge.

skill_tested: Drawing electric field direction for positive charge
question_text: Electric field lines around an isolated positive point charge point
answer: radially outward from the charge

choices.text: radially outward from the charge
radially inward toward the charge
in closed circular loops
only horizontally to the right

explanation: Positive source charges have field lines directed away from the charge.
common_mistake: Students reverse field line direction for positive charges.

skill_tested: Interpreting electric field line density
question_text: At a location where electric field lines are drawn closer together, the electric field strength is
answer: greater than where the lines are farther apart

choices.text: greater than where the lines are farther apart
weaker because lines crowd near weak fields
zero because lines never cross
independent of line spacing

explanation: Line density indicates field magnitude; crowding means stronger field.
common_mistake: Students think crowded lines indicate weaker fields or confuse spacing with direction.

skill_tested: Stating charge quantization from Millikan's experiment
question_text: Millikan's oil-drop experiment provided evidence that electric charge is
answer: quantized in multiples of a fundamental unit

choices.text: quantized in multiples of a fundamental unit
continuously variable at any value
always equal to the charge on a proton only
produced solely by friction

explanation: Measured droplet charges were integer multiples of $e \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$.
common_mistake: Students think charge can take any real value because macroscopic charge seems continuous.

skill_tested: Explaining force balance in Millikan oil-drop setup
question_text: In Millikan's apparatus, balancing a charged oil droplet in a uniform electric field requires adjusting the field until
answer: the upward electric force equals the downward gravitational force

choices.text: the upward electric force equals the downward gravitational force
the droplet accelerates upward indefinitely
magnetic force cancels gravity
the droplet becomes neutral

explanation: the upward electric force equals the downward gravitational force follows from the physics principles in outcome B2.10k (Forces and Fields). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students think balance means net force is maximized rather than zero.

skill_tested: Determining force direction on proton in uniform field
question_text: Two parallel conducting plates separated by $0.010\ \text{m}$ maintain a uniform electric field of $4.0 \times 10^4\ \text{N/C}$ pointing from Plate P ($+$) to Plate N ($-$). The electric force on a proton located between the plates points
answer: from Plate P toward Plate N

choices.text: from Plate P toward Plate N
from Plate N toward Plate P
perpendicular to both plates
with no force because the proton is neutral

explanation: A positive charge experiences force parallel to $\vec{E}$, from higher to lower potential.
common_mistake: Students apply force opposite to field for positive charges.

skill_tested: Relating electric potential energy to charge separation
question_text: The electric potential energy of two like charges held at fixed separation increases when
answer: the charges are moved farther apart against the repulsive electric force

choices.text: the charges are moved farther apart against the repulsive electric force
the charges are moved closer together
the charges are replaced by neutral spheres
the distance is held constant

explanation: Work done against the field increases stored electric potential energy.
common_mistake: Students think repelling charges lose potential energy when separated.

skill_tested: Analyzing field superposition between like charges
question_text: A student maps the electric field around two equal positive charges using a positive test charge. The field at the midpoint between the charges is
answer: zero because the two field contributions cancel

choices.text: zero because the two field contributions cancel
maximum because both fields add
directed toward one charge only
undefined because fields cannot overlap

explanation: Equal-magnitude opposite-direction contributions cancel at the midpoint.
common_mistake: Students add magnitudes without considering vector directions.

skill_tested: Explaining polarization in neutral insulators
question_text: Polarization of a neutral insulator near a charged object occurs because
answer: charges within atoms shift slightly, creating separated positive and negative regions

choices.text: charges within atoms shift slightly, creating separated positive and negative regions
protons permanently leave the material
electrons are destroyed
the material gains net charge without any external influence

explanation: Induced dipoles align with the external field without net charge transfer.
common_mistake: Students think polarization requires net charge on the neutral object.

skill_tested: Interpreting electroscope leaf divergence after induction
question_text: In a lab charging a neutral electroscope by induction with a negative rod, the leaves diverge because
answer: like charges repel and accumulate on the leaves after grounding steps are completed

choices.text: like charges repel and accumulate on the leaves after grounding steps are completed
protons accumulate on the leaves
gravity pulls the leaves apart
the rod removes all electrons from the electroscope

explanation: Induction redistributes electrons so the leaves share the same sign and repel.
common_mistake: Students think leaves separate due to attraction to the rod rather than leaf-leaf repulsion.

skill_tested: Representing uniform field between parallel plates
question_text: A uniform electric field between parallel plates is best represented by field lines that are
answer: parallel, equally spaced, and perpendicular to the plates

choices.text: parallel, equally spaced, and perpendicular to the plates
radiating outward from the centre of the gap
forming closed loops between plates
curving to meet at the plate edges only

explanation: Large parallel plates produce nearly uniform $\vec{E}$ except near edges.
common_mistake: Students draw radial patterns for all electric field situations.

skill_tested: Describing magnetic field geometry around straight wire
question_text: A magnetic field around a long straight current-carrying wire forms
answer: concentric circular field lines in planes perpendicular to the wire

choices.text: concentric circular field lines in planes perpendicular to the wire
radial lines pointing away from the wire like electric fields
parallel lines along the wire only
no field unless the wire is coiled

explanation: Ampère's law for a straight wire gives circular $\vec{B}$ centred on the conductor.
common_mistake: Students draw radial magnetic fields analogous to point charge electric fields.

skill_tested: Applying right-hand rule for straight current-carrying wire
question_text: The right-hand rule for a straight conductor: thumb points along conventional current and curled fingers indicate
answer: the direction of the magnetic field around the wire

choices.text: the direction of the magnetic field around the wire
the direction of electric field inside the wire
the force on a stationary proton nearby
the direction electrons flow inside the wire

explanation: Thumb = current; fingers curl in the $\vec{B}$ direction for straight wires.
common_mistake: Students point thumb along electron flow without converting to conventional current.

skill_tested: Determining magnetic force direction on moving positive charge
question_text: A proton moves east through a uniform magnetic field directed downward. Using the right-hand rule for positive charges, the magnetic force on the proton points
answer: south

choices.text: south
north (along the positive y-axis in the diagram)
upward (opposite to the direction of gravity)
east (perpendicular to the plane of the page, to the right)

explanation: Fingers = $\vec{v}$ (east), curl toward $\vec{B}$ (down); thumb points south.
common_mistake: Students reverse the cross-product order or use left hand for positive charges.

skill_tested: Stating magnetic force magnitude for perpendicular motion
question_text: The magnitude of magnetic force on a charge $q$ moving at speed $v$ perpendicular to uniform magnetic field $B$ is given by
answer: $F = qvB$

choices.text: $F = qvB$
confusing conduction and induction charging methods
assigning field direction opposite to force on a positive test charge
using classical orbit models to explain line spectra without quantization

explanation: Maximum force occurs at $90^\circ$ between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$: $F = qvB\sin\theta$ with $\sin 90^\circ = 1$.
common_mistake: Students invert the relationship or add an extra factor of $B$.

skill_tested: Recognizing zero magnetic force for parallel motion
question_text: A charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field parallel to the field lines. The magnetic force on the particle is
answer: zero because $\sin 0^\circ = 0$

choices.text: zero because $\sin 0^\circ = 0$
maximum because speed is highest
directed opposite to the velocity
equal to $qvB$ regardless of angle

explanation: $F = qvB\sin\theta$; parallel motion gives $\theta = 0$ and zero force.
common_mistake: Students assume any charged particle in any magnetic field experiences $qvB$.

skill_tested: Explaining velocity selector force balance condition
question_text: In a velocity selector, particles pass through crossed electric and magnetic fields adjusted so that
answer: electric and magnetic forces on selected-speed particles cancel

choices.text: electric and magnetic forces on selected-speed particles cancel
only gravitational force balances magnetic force
magnetic force always exceeds electric force
electric field is parallel to magnetic field

explanation: Selected particles have $qE = qvB$, allowing undeflected passage.
common_mistake: Students think the selector accelerates all particles equally rather than filtering by speed.

skill_tested: Determining velocity direction in velocity selector
question_text: For a positive ion to pass straight through a velocity selector with $\vec{E}$ upward and $\vec{B}$ into the page, the required velocity must be directed
answer: to the right

choices.text: to the right
comparing fission and fusion energy per reaction instead of per unit mass
upward (opposite to the direction of gravity)
into the page

explanation: $\vec{F}_E$ is down; $\vec{F}_B$ must be up, requiring $\vec{v}$ right by right-hand rule.
common_mistake: Students choose velocity parallel to $\vec{E}$ or $\vec{B}$ instead of perpendicular to both.

skill_tested: Identifying orientation for maximum force on current-carrying wire
question_text: A current-carrying wire in a uniform external magnetic field experiences maximum force when the wire is
answer: perpendicular to the field direction

choices.text: perpendicular to the field direction
parallel to the field direction
shielded by insulation only
stationary with no current

explanation: perpendicular to the field direction follows from the physics principles in outcome B3.8k (Forces and Fields). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students think force is greatest when wire is parallel to $\vec{B}$.

skill_tested: Applying force law for current-carrying conductor
question_text: The force on a straight wire of length $L$ carrying current $I$ perpendicular to magnetic field $B$ is
answer: $F = BIL$

choices.text: $F = BIL$
dividing by one mass only in two-body momentum problems
confusing impulse with kinetic energy in collision analysis
treating magnetic poles using electrostatic attraction rules

explanation: Wire force law parallels charge force: $F = BIL\sin\theta$ with $\sin 90^\circ = 1$.
common_mistake: Students confuse wire force with point-charge $qvB$ without including length.

skill_tested: Explaining galvanometer operation using motor principle
question_text: A galvanometer can detect current because a magnetic field exerts torque on
answer: a coil carrying current placed in the field

choices.text: a coil carrying current placed in the field
stationary neutral atoms in the coil
the insulation on the connecting wires only
the battery's chemical energy directly

explanation: Current in a coil experiences $\vec{B}$-induced torque proportional to $I$.
common_mistake: Students think magnetic fields push electrons without involving coil orientation.

skill_tested: Predicting force between parallel current-carrying wires
question_text: Two parallel wires carrying currents in the same direction
answer: attract each other

choices.text: attract each other
repel each other
experience no force
exchange electric charge

explanation: Each wire's field exerts force on the other's current; same-direction currents attract.
common_mistake: Students apply like-current repulsion rule incorrectly.

skill_tested: Relating compass behaviour to Earth's magnetic field
question_text: The Earth's magnetic field near the surface is useful for navigation because a
answer: compass needle aligns with the local magnetic field direction

choices.text: compass needle aligns with the local magnetic field direction
compass measures electric field from the ionosphere
compass detects gravitational field variations
compass works only at the magnetic equator

explanation: A freely pivoted magnet aligns parallel to $\vec{B}_{Earth}$.
common_mistake: Students think compasses find geographic north without magnetic field alignment.

skill_tested: Predicting trajectory of charge in perpendicular magnetic field
question_text: A charged particle moving in a plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field follows
answer: a circular path at constant speed

choices.text: a circular path at constant speed
a straight line accelerating forward
a parabolic path like projectile motion
a spiral that immediately stops

explanation: Magnetic force is always perpendicular to $\vec{v}$, providing centripetal acceleration only.
common_mistake: Students confuse magnetic deflection with gravitational projectile paths.

skill_tested: Relating solenoid field strength to current
question_text: Increasing the current in a solenoid while keeping the same number of turns and core material generally
answer: strengthens the magnetic field inside the solenoid

choices.text: strengthens the magnetic field inside the solenoid
eliminates the magnetic field
reverses only the electric field outside
has no effect on magnetic field strength

explanation: Solenoid field is proportional to current: $B \propto I$ for fixed geometry.
common_mistake: Students think field depends only on number of turns, ignoring current.

skill_tested: Drawing external magnetic field lines for bar magnet
question_text: A laboratory magnet labeled with N and S poles creates field lines that outside the magnet run
answer: from north pole to south pole through the surrounding space

choices.text: from north pole to south pole through the surrounding space
from south to north externally only
only inside the magnet with no external field
perpendicular to all iron filings everywhere

explanation: External field lines leave north and enter south, forming continuous loops through the magnet.
common_mistake: Students think lines start at south externally or exist only inside the material.

skill_tested: Linking induced current to mechanical energy input
question_text: When a conductor moves through a magnetic field and experiences an induced current, energy is transferred by
answer: the mechanical work done moving the conductor against magnetic forces

choices.text: the mechanical work done moving the conductor against magnetic forces
creating charge from nothing inside the conductor
increasing the conductor's mass
eliminating the magnetic field permanently

explanation: Generator effect converts mechanical input to electrical energy via changing flux.
common_mistake: Students think induced current appears without energy input.

skill_tested: Stating Faraday's law dependence on flux change rate
question_text: Faraday's law states that the magnitude of induced emf is proportional to
answer: the rate of change of magnetic flux through the loop

choices.text: the rate of change of magnetic flux through the loop
the total flux regardless of time
the square of the magnetic field only
the wire's resistance alone

explanation: $\mathcal{E} = -N\,\Delta\Phi/\Delta t$; changing flux induces emf.
common_mistake: Students think constant nonzero flux always induces emf.

skill_tested: Applying Lenz's law to oppose flux change
question_text: Lenz's law determines the direction of induced current such that the induced magnetic field
answer: opposes the change in external flux that caused it

choices.text: opposes the change in external flux that caused it
always aligns with the external field
always doubles the external flux change
is unrelated to flux change

explanation: Induced effects oppose the change producing them, consistent with energy conservation.
common_mistake: Students think induced field always aids the original flux change.

skill_tested: Predicting repulsion when magnet approaches coil
question_text: A bar magnet is pushed toward a conducting coil. By Lenz's law, the coil's induced magnetic field near the approaching pole will
answer: repel the approaching magnet

choices.text: repel the approaching magnet
attract the magnet more strongly
have no interaction with the magnet
cancel Earth's magnetic field completely

explanation: Coil creates field opposing increasing flux — repulsion slows the approach.
common_mistake: Students predict attraction, thinking induced poles always match approaching poles.

skill_tested: Explaining motor operation via torque on current coil
question_text: In a simple electric motor, a current-carrying coil in a magnetic field experiences
answer: a torque that can produce continuous rotation when commutated

choices.text: a torque that can produce continuous rotation when commutated
no force because the coil is symmetric
only gravitational torque
uniform acceleration along the field lines

explanation: Motor effect: $\vec{B}$ exerts force on current segments, producing torque about the axis.
common_mistake: Students confuse motor torque with generator induction.

skill_tested: Describing commutator role in DC motor
question_text: A split-ring commutator in a DC motor reverses current in the coil every half rotation so that
answer: torque continues to drive rotation in the same direction

choices.text: torque continues to drive rotation in the same direction
the coil stops after one half turn
magnetic field direction reverses instead
electric field inside the coil disappears

explanation: Commutation flips current direction as torque would otherwise reverse.
common_mistake: Students think commutators measure induced emf rather than reverse supply current.

skill_tested: Describing generator energy conversion via flux change
question_text: An electric generator converts mechanical energy to electrical energy by
answer: rotating a coil in a magnetic field to change magnetic flux through the coil

choices.text: rotating a coil in a magnetic field to change magnetic flux through the coil
passing constant flux through a stationary coil
heating the coil until electrons evaporate
storing charge on the commutator permanently

explanation: Changing flux induces emf; rotation provides continuous flux change.
common_mistake: Students think generators create energy without mechanical input.

skill_tested: Explaining sinusoidal AC output from rotating coil
question_text: The output of an AC generator varies sinusoidally because the induced emf depends on
answer: the changing angle between the coil plane and the magnetic field during rotation

choices.text: the changing angle between the coil plane and the magnetic field during rotation
a constant flux always producing constant emf
only the coil's resistance changing
gravitational potential energy of the coil

explanation: $\mathcal{E} \propto \sin(\omega t)$ as flux linkage varies with rotation.
common_mistake: Students expect DC output from any rotating coil without commutator.

skill_tested: Explaining electromagnetic braking via eddy currents
question_text: Eddy currents induced in a metal plate moving through a magnetic field can
answer: oppose the motion by magnetic interaction, acting as electromagnetic braking

choices.text: oppose the motion by magnetic interaction, acting as electromagnetic braking
accelerate the plate without energy loss
eliminate all magnetic field in the region
flow only in non-metallic materials

explanation: Lenz's law: eddy currents create fields opposing motion, dissipating kinetic energy.
common_mistake: Students think induced currents always speed up the moving conductor.

skill_tested: Applying transformer turns ratio to voltage
question_text: A transformer steps voltage up on the secondary coil compared to the primary when the secondary has
answer: more turns than the primary

choices.text: more turns than the primary
fewer turns than the primary
the same turns with no core
no magnetic flux linking the coils

explanation: $V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p$ for ideal transformers; more secondary turns gives higher secondary voltage.
common_mistake: Students invert the turns ratio or think transformers create power.

skill_tested: Distinguishing motor input from generator output
question_text: In a motor versus generator comparison using the same coil and field, the motor requires
answer: an external current supply to produce rotation

choices.text: an external current supply to produce rotation
only mechanical rotation with no electrical input
a permanent absence of magnetic flux
static charges on the commutator only

explanation: Motors convert electrical to mechanical; generators do the reverse.
common_mistake: Students cannot distinguish which device needs external electrical input.

skill_tested: Predicting galvanometer reversal when motion reverses
question_text: A student moves a magnet into a solenoid connected to a galvanometer and observes deflection. Reversing the direction of motion without changing speed
answer: reverses the galvanometer deflection direction

choices.text: reverses the galvanometer deflection direction
produces identical deflection
produces zero deflection
triples the resistance of the solenoid

explanation: Opposite $\Delta\Phi/\Delta t$ sign reverses induced emf polarity per Lenz's law.
common_mistake: Students think speed alone sets direction, ignoring motion direction relative to coil.

skill_tested: Applying velocity selector equation $v = E/B$
question_text: A particle with speed $2.0 \times 10^6\ \text{m/s}$ enters a velocity selector where $E = 4.0 \times 10^4\ \text{N/C}$ and $B = 0.020\ \text{T}$. For the particle to pass undeflected, the required speed satisfies
answer: $v = E/B$

choices.text: $v = E/B$
$v = EB$ (incorrect inversion of the velocity selector ratio)
$v = B/E$ (inverted velocity selector equation)
confusing diffraction with interference in wave optics

explanation: Balance gives $v = E/B = (4.0 \times 10^4)/(0.020) = 2.0 \times 10^6\ \text{m/s}$.
common_mistake: Students multiply $E$ and $B$ or invert the ratio.

skill_tested: Identifying orientation for maximum torque on current loop
question_text: When analyzing force on a current loop in a uniform magnetic field, the maximum torque occurs when the plane of the loop is
answer: parallel to the magnetic field

choices.text: parallel to the magnetic field
perpendicular to the field
aligned so no current flows
removed from the field entirely

explanation: Torque is maximum when magnetic moment is perpendicular to $\vec{B}$ (loop plane parallel to field).
common_mistake: Students confuse loop plane orientation with magnetic moment orientation.

skill_tested: Determining electric force direction on negative droplet
question_text: A negatively charged oil droplet in Millikan's experiment is held stationary between horizontal plates with the upper plate positive. The electric force on the droplet acts
answer: upward toward the positive plate

choices.text: upward toward the positive plate
downward toward the negative plate
horizontally between the plates
with zero magnitude at balance

explanation: Field points from the positive plate downward; a negative charge experiences force opposite to $\vec{E}$, so upward.
common_mistake: Students apply force direction for positive charges to negative droplets.

skill_tested: Explaining motional emf from changing loop area
question_text: A conducting rod slides on rails perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field. An induced current circulates if the rod moves because
answer: the area enclosed by the loop changes, changing magnetic flux

choices.text: the area enclosed by the loop changes, changing magnetic flux
the rod's mass changes
the magnetic field reverses spontaneously
static charge appears without flux change

explanation: Motional emf arises from changing flux through the expanding or contracting loop.
common_mistake: Students think any motion in a field induces current without flux change.

skill_tested: Comparing forces on proton and electron in uniform field
question_text: A proton and an electron at the same distance from a large positive plate in a parallel-plate capacitor experience electric forces that are
answer: equal in magnitude but opposite in direction

choices.text: equal in magnitude but opposite in direction
equal in magnitude and the same direction
zero for the electron only
independent of plate charge

explanation: Same $|q|$ and same $E$ give equal $F$ magnitudes; opposite signs give opposite directions.
common_mistake: Students think the lighter electron must experience smaller electric force.

skill_tested: Calculating Coulomb force between two point charges
question_text: Two point charges of $+3.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$ and $-3.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$ are separated by $0.30\ \text{m}$. Using $k = 9.0 \times 10^9\ \text{N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$, what is the magnitude of the electric force in newtons? Record to two significant figures.
common_mistake: Students forget to square the distance or mishandle scientific notation exponents.

skill_tested: Computing electric force on proton in uniform field
question_text: A uniform electric field of $2.5 \times 10^3\ \text{N/C}$ acts on a proton. What is the magnitude of the electric force in newtons? Use $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. Record in scientific notation as $a \times 10^b$ with one digit before the decimal.
common_mistake: Students multiply exponents incorrectly or use electron mass instead of charge.

skill_tested: Calculating magnetic force on moving electron
question_text: An electron travels at $3.0 \times 10^6\ \text{m/s}$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of $0.15\ \text{T}$. What is the magnitude of the magnetic force in newtons? Use $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$. Record in scientific notation as $a \times 10^b$.
common_mistake: Students omit the charge, divide by $B$, or fail to use perpendicular condition.

skill_tested: Calculating force on current-carrying wire in magnetic field
question_text: A straight wire $0.25\ \text{m}$ long carries $4.0\ \text{A}$ perpendicular to a $0.80\ \text{T}$ magnetic field. What is the magnetic force on the wire in newtons? Record to two significant figures.
explanation: Apply the relationship from outcome B3.8k in Forces and Fields. Substituting the given values yields 0.80, with consistent units and significant figures.
common_mistake: Students use $qvB$ instead of $BIL$ or forget to include wire length.

skill_tested: Describing transverse nature of EM fields
question_text: In the classical wave model of electromagnetic radiation, the electric and magnetic field components oscillate
answer: perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation

choices.text: perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation
parallel to the direction of propagation only
in the same direction as each other along the ray path
only when the radiation passes through a vacuum

explanation: Transverse EM waves have mutually perpendicular $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ fields, both normal to propagation.
common_mistake: Students describe EM fields as longitudinal like sound in air.

skill_tested: Stating constant speed of EM waves in vacuum
question_text: All electromagnetic waves in vacuum share the same
answer: speed of $3.00 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ regardless of frequency

choices.text: speed of $3.00 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ regardless of frequency
wavelength, which is fixed for every type of radiation
period, which is identical for radio and gamma rays
amplitude, which must be constant in vacuum

explanation: In vacuum, $c = f\lambda$ is constant; changing frequency changes wavelength inversely.
common_mistake: Students think higher-frequency radiation travels faster in vacuum.

skill_tested: Applying inverse proportion of frequency and wavelength
question_text: The relationship $c = f\lambda$ implies that doubling the frequency of an EM wave in vacuum
answer: halves its wavelength while speed remains unchanged

choices.text: halves its wavelength while speed remains unchanged
doubles its wavelength and doubles its speed
leaves wavelength unchanged
quadruples its speed

explanation: With $c$ fixed, $f$ and $\lambda$ are inversely proportional.
common_mistake: Students treat frequency and wavelength as directly proportional.

skill_tested: Identifying high-frequency end of EM spectrum
question_text: X-rays and gamma rays are grouped at the high-energy end of the spectrum primarily because they have
answer: very high frequencies and very short wavelengths

choices.text: very high frequencies and very short wavelengths
lower speed in vacuum than infrared radiation
longer wavelengths than ultraviolet light
magnetic fields but no oscillating electric fields

explanation: Photon energy and wave frequency are highest for ionizing short-wavelength radiation.
common_mistake: Students select X-rays based on speed differences in vacuum.

skill_tested: Explaining polarization as evidence for transverse waves
question_text: Polarizing sunglasses reduce glare from horizontal surfaces mainly by
answer: blocking light whose electric field oscillates in a selected plane

choices.text: blocking light whose electric field oscillates in a selected plane
increasing the speed of reflected light in glass
converting transverse waves into longitudinal waves
absorbing all wavelengths equally without orientation effect

explanation: Polarizers transmit one polarization plane and absorb the orthogonal component.
common_mistake: Students think polarization changes the frequency of reflected light.

skill_tested: Relating wave amplitude to radiation intensity
question_text: For an electromagnetic wave, doubling the amplitude of the oscillating electric field typically
answer: quadruples the intensity of the radiation

choices.text: quadruples the intensity of the radiation
doubles the intensity only
does not affect intensity at all
halves the wavelength in vacuum

explanation: Intensity is proportional to the square of amplitude for sinusoidal waves.
common_mistake: Students assume intensity scales linearly with amplitude.

skill_tested: Justifying X-ray selection for medical imaging
question_text: A medical imaging team chooses X-rays instead of radio waves to image bone because X-rays
answer: have wavelengths short enough to resolve fine internal structures

choices.text: have wavelengths short enough to resolve fine internal structures
travel slower through tissue so they map density directly
are longitudinal and penetrate solids more easily
have lower photon energy and therefore pass through bone

explanation: Short-wavelength radiation improves resolution and interacts strongly with dense matter.
common_mistake: Students think radio waves are chosen for imaging because they are safer due to higher energy.

skill_tested: Applying law of reflection with normal reference
question_text: When a light ray strikes a smooth plane mirror, the angle of reflection is measured between
answer: the reflected ray and the normal to the surface

choices.text: the reflected ray and the normal to the surface
the incident ray and the mirror surface only
the reflected ray and the mirror surface only
the incident and reflected rays with no reference to the normal

explanation: Both incidence and reflection angles are defined relative to the normal.
common_mistake: Students measure angles from the mirror surface instead of the normal.

skill_tested: Distinguishing specular and diffuse reflection
question_text: Diffuse reflection from rough paper differs from specular reflection from a mirror because rough surfaces
answer: reflect parallel incident rays in many directions

choices.text: reflect parallel incident rays in many directions
do not obey the law of reflection at each micro-facet
absorb all light and emit none
reverse the frequency of incoming light

explanation: Each tiny facet obeys reflection locally, but surface irregularities scatter rays.
common_mistake: Students think diffuse reflection violates the law of reflection.

skill_tested: Defining index of refraction
question_text: The index of refraction $n$ of a transparent medium is defined as
answer: the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to the speed in the medium

choices.text: the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to the speed in the medium
the ratio of the angle of incidence to the angle of refraction
the product of frequency and wavelength in the medium
the sine of the critical angle only

explanation: the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to the speed in the medium follows from the physics principles in outcome C1.6k (Electromagnetic Radiation). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students confuse $n$ with Snell's law ratio $\sin\theta_i/\sin\theta_r$.

skill_tested: Explaining refraction toward normal in denser medium
question_text: A light ray entering glass from air bends toward the normal because in glass the light
answer: travels slower while its frequency stays essentially unchanged

choices.text: travels slower while its frequency stays essentially unchanged
speeds up while wavelength decreases
changes frequency to match the medium
maintains the same direction as in air

explanation: Reduced speed with fixed frequency means shorter wavelength and refraction toward the normal.
common_mistake: Students think refraction occurs because frequency changes at the boundary.

skill_tested: Predicting refraction direction using relative index
question_text: According to Snell's law, if light passes from medium A ($n = 1.33$) into medium B ($n = 1.50$), the refracted ray in B will
answer: bend toward the normal compared with the incident direction in A

choices.text: bend toward the normal compared with the incident direction in A
bend away from the normal because it speeds up
travel undeflected regardless of angle
reflect completely for every angle of incidence

explanation: Entering a higher-index medium slows light and bends it toward the normal.
common_mistake: Students predict bending away from the normal whenever a ray enters a new medium.

skill_tested: Stating conditions for total internal reflection
question_text: Total internal reflection can occur only when light attempts to travel from
answer: a higher-index medium into a lower-index medium at angles beyond the critical angle

choices.text: a higher-index medium into a lower-index medium at angles beyond the critical angle
any medium into vacuum at very small angles of incidence
air into glass at all angles of incidence
a lower-index medium into a higher-index medium

explanation: TIR requires incidence in the optically denser medium with angle exceeding $\theta_c$.
common_mistake: Students think TIR happens whenever light hits any transparent boundary.

skill_tested: Explaining optical fibre operation using TIR
question_text: Optical fibre communication relies on repeated total internal reflection so that light pulses
answer: remain guided inside the core with minimal loss through the cladding

choices.text: remain guided inside the core with minimal loss through the cladding
gain speed in the cladding to reach distant receivers
diffract outward at every bend to broadcast signals
change frequency each time they reflect internally

explanation: Core-cladding index contrast traps rays by TIR along curved paths.
common_mistake: Students think fibre optics works by simple mirror reflection at the outer glass surface.

skill_tested: Explaining apparent depth with refraction
question_text: A fisherman sees a fish closer to the surface than its actual depth because light from the fish
answer: bends away from the normal when leaving water, making the image appear shallower

choices.text: bends away from the normal when leaving water, making the image appear shallower
speeds up entering water and focuses to a deeper image
is completely absorbed before reaching the eye
undergoes total internal reflection at the water surface always

explanation: Refraction at the water-air interface produces a virtual image above the real object.
common_mistake: Students think the fish looks deeper because light slows in air.

skill_tested: Matching converging lens to convex terminology
question_text: A converging thin lens is also described as
answer: convex

choices.text: convex
concave (curved inward, for mirrors only)
diverging (spreads light rays apart, for lenses)
plane (flat surface with no curvature)

explanation: Converging lenses are thicker at the centre and have convex surfaces.
common_mistake: Students pair converging with concave because both words contain letter patterns they confuse.

skill_tested: Matching diverging lens to concave terminology
question_text: A diverging thin lens is also described as
answer: concave

choices.text: concave
convex (curved outward, for mirrors only)
dividing by one mass only in two-body momentum problems
parabolic mirror

explanation: Diverging lenses are thinner at the centre and have concave surfaces.
common_mistake: Students label diverging lenses as convex.

skill_tested: Identifying concave mirror as converging
question_text: A concave mirror used in a shaving mirror acts as a
answer: converging mirror

choices.text: converging mirror
diverging mirror only
plane mirror with infinite focal length always
lens that refracts rather than reflects

explanation: Concave mirrors converge parallel rays toward a real focal point.
common_mistake: Students confuse concave with diverging because both terms sound similar.

skill_tested: Identifying convex mirror as diverging
question_text: A convex rear-view vehicle mirror is a
answer: diverging mirror that produces a smaller, wider field of view

choices.text: diverging mirror that produces a smaller, wider field of view
converging mirror that magnifies distant traffic
plane mirror with no curvature
converging lens mounted on the bumper

explanation: Convex mirrors spread reflected rays and form virtual upright reduced images.
common_mistake: Students think convex mirrors magnify like converging lenses.

skill_tested: Predicting real image location for converging lens
question_text: An object placed beyond $2f$ from a converging lens typically produces
answer: a real, inverted image between $f$ and $2f$ on the opposite side

choices.text: a real, inverted image between $f$ and $2f$ on the opposite side
a virtual upright image on the same side as the object
no image because the lens cannot refract parallel rays
a real upright image exactly at the object location

explanation: Standard ray rules place a real inverted image inside the $f$-$2f$ image region.
common_mistake: Students predict virtual images for all converging-lens setups.

skill_tested: Predicting virtual magnified image inside focal length
question_text: When an object is placed inside the focal length of a converging lens, the image formed is
answer: virtual, upright, and larger than the object

choices.text: virtual, upright, and larger than the object
real, inverted, and smaller than the object
real and located on the same side as the object
absent because rays never refract

explanation: Inside $f$, diverging emergent rays appear to originate from a virtual image.
common_mistake: Students apply the beyond-$2f$ image rules to all object distances.

skill_tested: Describing images formed by diverging lenses
question_text: A diverging lens always forms images that are
answer: virtual, upright, and smaller than the object

choices.text: virtual, upright, and smaller than the object
real, inverted, and magnified
real and located on the opposite side
nonexistent for any object distance

explanation: Diverging lenses spread rays so only virtual reduced images are produced.
common_mistake: Students think diverging lenses can project real images onto a screen.

skill_tested: Applying parallel-ray rule for converging lens
question_text: In a ray diagram for a converging lens, a ray parallel to the principal axis after refraction passes through
answer: the focal point on the opposite side of the lens

choices.text: the focal point on the opposite side of the lens
the centre of the lens at zero deviation only
a point twice the focal length on the same side
the normal to the lens surface at the vertex

explanation: Parallel-ray rule is the primary construction line for thin lenses.
common_mistake: Students draw parallel rays continuing undeviated through the lens.

skill_tested: Defining diffraction of electromagnetic waves
question_text: Diffraction of light is best described as
answer: the spreading of waves when they pass through an opening or around an edge

choices.text: the spreading of waves when they pass through an opening or around an edge
the alignment of wave crests from two separate coherent sources
the complete reflection of light at a boundary above the critical angle
the conversion of photons into electrons at a metal surface

explanation: Diffraction involves bending and spreading due to aperture or obstacle size.
common_mistake: Students label double-slit bright fringes as diffraction rather than interference.

skill_tested: Defining interference by wave superposition
question_text: Interference of light occurs when
answer: two or more coherent waves overlap and combine by superposition

choices.text: two or more coherent waves overlap and combine by superposition
a single ray reflects once from a smooth mirror
light slows down entering a denser medium
photons eject electrons above a threshold frequency

explanation: Interference requires overlapping waves with a stable phase relationship.
common_mistake: Students describe any bright beam as interference without superposition.

skill_tested: Interpreting double-slit pattern as interference
question_text: In a double-slit experiment with monochromatic light, equally spaced bright fringes on a screen indicate
answer: constructive interference at path differences equal to whole wavelengths

choices.text: constructive interference at path differences equal to whole wavelengths
diffraction from a single narrow slit only
polarization rotation in the slits
photoelectric emission from the screen

explanation: Young's pattern is an interference signature from two coherent sources.
common_mistake: Students attribute double-slit fringes solely to single-slit diffraction.

skill_tested: Distinguishing single-slit diffraction from double-slit interference
question_text: A single-slit diffraction pattern differs from a double-slit interference pattern because the single-slit pattern
answer: has a broad central maximum with weaker side maxima

choices.text: has a broad central maximum with weaker side maxima
shows no central bright region at all
contains only equally spaced fringes of equal brightness
requires two coherent sources separated by many metres

explanation: Single-slit diffraction envelopes produce one dominant central peak.
common_mistake: Students expect identical fringe spacing and brightness for both arrangements.

skill_tested: Stating path-difference condition for constructive interference
question_text: Two coherent sources produce constructive interference at a point where the path difference equals
answer: an integer multiple of the wavelength

choices.text: an integer multiple of the wavelength
half-integer multiples of the wavelength only
exactly one quarter wavelength always
zero frequency difference only

explanation: an integer multiple of the wavelength follows from the physics principles in outcome C1.12k (Electromagnetic Radiation). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students use $\lambda/2$ for constructive instead of destructive interference.

skill_tested: Stating path-difference condition for destructive interference
question_text: Destructive interference between two coherent light waves occurs when the path difference is
answer: an odd integer multiple of half a wavelength

choices.text: an odd integer multiple of half a wavelength
any integer multiple of one full wavelength
zero for all points on a screen
equal to twice the amplitude of either wave

explanation: Destructive condition: $\Delta path = (m + \tfrac{1}{2})\lambda$.
common_mistake: Students apply the constructive condition to destructive cases.

skill_tested: Explaining thin-film interference colours
question_text: Thin-film colours on soap bubbles arise primarily because reflected waves from the top and bottom film surfaces
answer: interfere constructively or destructively depending on film thickness and wavelength

choices.text: interfere constructively or destructively depending on film thickness and wavelength
diffract around the bubble without reflection
change polarization so no colour is visible
increase the speed of light in air surrounding the bubble

explanation: Path differences inside thin films produce wavelength-dependent interference colours.
common_mistake: Students attribute soap colours only to dispersion without interference.

skill_tested: Predicting effect of slit separation on fringe spacing
question_text: In a laboratory double-slit setup, increasing the slit separation while holding wavelength constant typically
answer: decreases the fringe spacing on the distant screen

choices.text: decreases the fringe spacing on the distant screen
increases the fringe spacing proportionally
eliminates all dark fringes immediately
changes the vacuum speed of the laser light

explanation: Fringe spacing is inversely related to slit separation for fixed screen distance.
common_mistake: Students predict wider slits always mean wider fringes.

skill_tested: Stating threshold frequency in photoelectric effect
question_text: The photoelectric effect demonstrates that electrons are ejected from a metal surface only when incident light
answer: has frequency above a material-specific threshold

choices.text: has frequency above a material-specific threshold
has arbitrarily low frequency if intensity is very high
is polarized parallel to the surface always
travels slower than sound in the metal

explanation: Below threshold frequency, no photoelectrons are emitted regardless of intensity.
common_mistake: Students believe bright low-frequency light will eventually eject electrons.

skill_tested: Distinguishing intensity effect on photoelectron count
question_text: In the photoelectric effect, increasing the intensity of light above the threshold frequency while keeping frequency fixed
answer: increases the number of photoelectrons emitted per second

choices.text: increases the number of photoelectrons emitted per second
increases the maximum kinetic energy of each photoelectron
lowers the work function of the metal
decreases the stopping potential required

explanation: Higher intensity means more photons per second, not higher photon energy.
common_mistake: Students think brighter light increases each electron's maximum kinetic energy.

skill_tested: Relating photon frequency to maximum photoelectron energy
question_text: Increasing the frequency of incident light above the threshold in a photoelectric experiment increases
answer: the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons

choices.text: the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons
only the number of electrons with zero kinetic energy
the work function of the metal surface
the wavelength of the metal's lattice spacing

explanation: Higher $f$ means higher photon energy, raising $E_{k,\max}$ via $hf = W + E_{k,\max}$.
common_mistake: Students expect intensity, not frequency, to control maximum kinetic energy.

skill_tested: Defining work function in photoelectric context
question_text: The work function of a metal is defined as
answer: the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the metal surface

choices.text: the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the metal surface
the kinetic energy of the fastest photoelectron always
the intensity of light needed to begin emission
the momentum of an incident photon divided by wavelength

explanation: $W$ is the binding energy threshold appearing in Einstein's photoelectric equation.
common_mistake: Students confuse work function with measured stopping potential directly.

skill_tested: Interpreting slope of photoelectric graph as Planck constant
question_text: Einstein's photoelectric equation $E_{k,\max} = hf - W$ shows that a graph of $E_{k,\max}$ versus frequency has slope equal to
answer: Planck's constant $h$

choices.text: Planck's constant $h$
the work function $W$
the speed of light $c$
the electron charge $e$

explanation: Planck's constant $h$ follows from the physics principles in outcome C2.4k (Electromagnetic Radiation). The other options reflect common diploma-level misconceptions on this concept.
common_mistake: Students interpret the intercept as slope or vice versa.

skill_tested: Explaining solar cell operation with photoelectric model
question_text: A solar cell converts sunlight to electrical energy using the photoelectric effect because photons with sufficient energy
answer: transfer energy to electrons that then move through an external circuit

choices.text: transfer energy to electrons that then move through an external circuit
increase the metal's work function continuously during the day
eliminate the need for any electric field in the device
reduce the frequency of incoming radiation inside the cell

explanation: Photon absorption liberates charge carriers that a built-in field drives through a circuit.
common_mistake: Students think solar cells store photons mechanically without electron transfer.

skill_tested: Stating photon energy formula
question_text: The energy of a single photon is calculated using
answer: $E = hf$

choices.text: $E = hf$
assuming photon intensity changes individual photon energy
confusing diffraction with interference in wave optics
$E = \lambda c$ without Planck's constant

explanation: Photon energy is proportional to frequency through Planck's constant.
common_mistake: Students invert the relationship and divide by frequency.

skill_tested: Comparing photon energies for different visible colours
question_text: A photon of blue light has higher energy than a photon of red light of the same intensity because blue light
answer: has higher frequency and shorter wavelength

choices.text: has higher frequency and shorter wavelength
travels faster in vacuum
has lower frequency and longer wavelength
consists of larger amplitude waves only with no frequency change

explanation: At fixed intensity, individual blue photons carry more energy due to higher $f$.
common_mistake: Students think colour energy depends only on brightness, not frequency.

skill_tested: Stating photon momentum relation
question_text: The momentum of a photon is given by
answer: $p = h/\lambda$

choices.text: $p = h/\lambda$
$p = h\lambda$
$p = mc^2$ for a photon with nonzero rest mass
treating inelastic collisions as conserving kinetic energy

explanation: Massless photons carry momentum inversely proportional to wavelength.
common_mistake: Students multiply $h$ and $\lambda$ instead of dividing.

skill_tested: Describing wavelength change in Compton scattering
question_text: In Compton scattering, an X-ray photon colliding with a nearly free electron emerges with
answer: longer wavelength and lower energy than before the collision

choices.text: longer wavelength and lower energy than before the collision
shorter wavelength because it gains speed in the electron
unchanged wavelength because photons cannot interact with matter
higher frequency after transferring energy to the electron

explanation: The photon transfers energy and momentum to the electron, reducing its own energy.
common_mistake: Students predict the photon gains energy during the collision.

skill_tested: Linking Compton shift to photon momentum model
question_text: Compton's experiments supported the particle model of light because the observed wavelength shift depended on
answer: scattering angle and matched predictions using photon momentum transfer

choices.text: scattering angle and matched predictions using photon momentum transfer
only the intensity of the incident beam
the polarization direction alone with no angle dependence
classical wave interference alone without electron recoil

explanation: The angle-dependent shift confirmed discrete photon momentum exchange.
common_mistake: Students think Compton shift arises from simple mirror reflection.

skill_tested: Identifying wave evidence for electromagnetic radiation
question_text: Evidence that light behaves as a wave includes
answer: interference and diffraction patterns produced without matter exchange

choices.text: interference and diffraction patterns produced without matter exchange
photoelectron emission proportional to intensity above threshold
wavelength increase after collision with a free electron
discrete photon energy proportional to frequency

explanation: Wave behaviour is shown by superposition phenomena such as Young's fringes.
common_mistake: Students list photoelectric or Compton results as wave evidence.

skill_tested: Identifying particle evidence for electromagnetic radiation
question_text: Evidence that light behaves as a particle includes
answer: the photoelectric effect and Compton scattering

choices.text: the photoelectric effect and Compton scattering
constructive interference in a double-slit experiment
polarization by a single filter
refraction at an air-glass boundary

explanation: Particle-like behaviour appears when energy and momentum transfer occur in quanta.
common_mistake: Students cite refraction or interference as proof of particle behaviour.

skill_tested: Evaluating wave-particle complementarity for light
question_text: A student claims that because light shows interference, it cannot show photoelectric behaviour. The best evaluation is that
answer: light exhibits both wave and particle models depending on the experiment

choices.text: light exhibits both wave and particle models depending on the experiment
the wave model alone explains all optical phenomena completely
the particle model alone replaces Maxwell's equations in every case
interference disproves the existence of photons entirely

explanation: Wave-particle complementarity means models are selected by experimental context.
common_mistake: Students treat wave and particle descriptions as mutually exclusive always.

skill_tested: Relating stopping potential to maximum photoelectron energy
question_text: A photoelectric experiment measures stopping potential increasing linearly with frequency above threshold because stopping potential is directly related to
answer: the maximum kinetic energy of the fastest emitted electrons

choices.text: the maximum kinetic energy of the fastest emitted electrons
the number of photons striking the surface per second only
the work function, which changes with each frequency
the wavelength of the metal lattice

explanation: Higher $E_{k,\max}$ requires greater retarding potential to stop the fastest electrons.
common_mistake: Students think stopping potential measures photocurrent count, not maximum energy.

skill_tested: Justifying laser eye protection by photon energy

occupied entirely by electrons only
